steps.title: Identify the doubling time parameter
steps.content: In $f(t)=40{,}000(2)^{t/790}$, the population doubles when the exponent equals $1$, i.e. when $t/790=1$.

steps.title: Apply to interpret the doubling time
steps.content: That gives $t=790$ minutes. That matches choice B — B is correct.

steps.title: Use the source rationale
steps.content: Choice B is correct. It's given that $t$ minutes after an initial observation, the number of bacteria in a population is $40,000{\left(2\right)}^{\frac{t}{790}}$. This expression consists of the initial number of bacteria, $40,000$, multiplied by the expression ${2}^{\frac{t}{790}}$. The time, in minutes, it takes for the number of bacteria to double is the increase in the value of $t$ that causes the expression ${2}^{\frac{t}{790}}$ to double. Since the base is $2$, the expression ${2}^{\frac{t}{790}}$ will double when the exponent increases by $1$. Since the exponent of this expression is $\frac{t}{790}$, the exponent will increase by $1$ when $t$ increases by $790$. Therefore, the time, in minutes, it takes for the number of bacteria in the population to double is $790$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice A is incorrect. This is the base of the exponent, not the time it takes for the number of bacteria in the population to double.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice C is incorrect. This is the number of minutes it takes for the population to double twice.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice D is incorrect. This is the number of bacteria that are initially observed, not the time it takes for the number of bacteria in the population to double.
